main_summary.summary: Julia Matilda Burns, interviewed in Tchula, Mississippi on March 13, 2013 by John Dittmer with videographer John Bishop, describes her trajectory from a Farm Security Administration homestead in Humphreys County to a career as a Holmes County teacher, school board member, and Civil Rights Movement collaborator. Born in Greenwood and raised on 123 FSA-purchased acres after her father moved the family from town to country in the early 1940s, Burns graduated from Humphreys County Training School in 1954 and earned an English degree at Mississippi Valley State under founding president James Herbert White. She student-taught in Belzoni, briefly moved to Chicago after marriage, and returned to Tchula in 1962, where principal S.B. Marshall hired her at Marshall High School. Burns describes operating behind the scenes during the SNCC and MFDP organizing years, hosting Freedom Summer workers including Henry and Sue Lorenzi, Abe Osheroff (who helped build the Mileston Community Center), and SNCC organizer John Ball from Greenwood. Her son was among the first-grade cohort that desegregated Tchula Consolidated School in 1965, after which the white population departed for Cruger-Tchula Academy. Elected to the Holmes County School Board around 1971, she also served as secretary of the Mileston Cooperative, coordinating Dr. Demitri Shimkin's University of Illinois Holmes County Health Research Program.
key_themes: Mileston Cooperative and SNCC support work
Black educator activism on school boards
main_summary.historical_significance:
Burns's testimony documents the intersection of Black landownership through FSA homesteading, Black educator activism during the segregation-to-integration transition, and academic-community partnerships between the University of Illinois and Holmes County's grassroots organizing infrastructure. Her account of operating as a teacher behind the scenes — hosting Freedom Summer workers at her home, feeding them lemonade and tea cakes, while keeping a public low profile to preserve her teaching position — preserves a specific organizational pattern that civil rights scholarship often overlooks.

Her firsthand account of her son's participation in the 1965 first-grade desegregation of Tchula Consolidated School, the school's serial burnings, and the white community's wholesale exit to Cruger-Tchula Academy provides primary-source detail on Holmes County's particular pattern of segregation academy formation. Her recollection of serving on the Holmes County School Board alongside Dr. Arenia C. Mallory of Saints Industrial School, and her role as secretary linking the Mileston Cooperative to Dr. Demitri Shimkin's Holmes County Health Research Program, document an underexamined network connecting cooperative organizing, public health research, and the Robert F. Kennedy economic development initiatives that followed his 1967 Mississippi Delta poverty tour.

chapters.title: FSA Homestead in Humphreys County
chapters.summary: Burns describes her family's move from Greenwood to Humphreys County around 1942, when her father purchased 123 acres through the Farm Security Administration. She recounts the family economy of cotton, corn, livestock, and self-sufficiency, with her mother's early death forcing her to take on household responsibility at thirteen.
related_events: Black landownership in the Mississippi Delta

chapters.title: Early Country Schools
chapters.summary: Burns describes attending a two-teacher, eight-grade country school with a pot-bellied stove, followed by the consolidated Womack and Hodge school and Humphreys County Training School in Louise. She notes that Mississippi made no equalization investment in Black schools before Brown v. Board of Education, with new textbooks appearing only in ninth grade alongside a new Everyday Science class.

chapters.title: Emmett Till and the 1950s Climate
chapters.summary: Burns recalls reading about Emmett Till's murder in Jet magazine while finishing high school in 1955, and describes her father warning her brother to stay clear of white girls in Belzoni. She notes that Attorney Boyd and other activists later cited the Till case as the catalyst for their movement involvement.

chapters.title: Church Life and Mentorship to College
chapters.summary: Burns describes alternating between the Methodist and Baptist congregations near her family's farm, with the family pastor visiting on the fourth Sunday of each month. She credits her tenth-grade English teacher with directing her toward an English-teaching career, and recalls reading Alfred Lord Tennyson and later Eldridge Cleaver's Soul on Ice.

chapters.title: Mississippi Valley State and Student Teaching
chapters.summary: Burns enrolled at Mississippi Valley State University around 1955-1956, when the institution had only two or three buildings and a class of sixteen to thirty-two students under founding president Dr. James Herbert White. She describes commuting by bus to college and completing her student teaching in Belzoni, where Clarice Reid was among her students.

chapters.title: Belzoni, Chicago, and Return to Tchula
chapters.summary: Burns describes Belzoni's reputation as a violent town that Black workers entered quickly and left quickly, with neighbor families and Civil Rights workers wary of overnight stays. She married, moved briefly to Chicago where her husband worked at Wieboldt's department store, then returned to Tchula in 1962 where principal S.B. Marshall hired her at Marshall High School that fall.
related_events: Belzoni's reputation among Civil Rights workers

chapters.title: Behind-the-Scenes Movement Work
chapters.summary: Burns describes how Greenwood SNCC organizer John Ball came to Marshall High School to recruit her, after which the principal warned she would lose her job if she continued. She hosted Freedom Summer workers, including Henry and Sue Lorenzi, at her home with refreshments after they parked behind the house to avoid being seen. She names Abe Osheroff, who helped build the Mileston Community Center, and Rev. Arrell Whitaker, Rev. Russell, F.D. Marshall, Bernice Mangum, and Emma Lou Williams as fellow Mileston-area organizers.

chapters.title: Tchula School Desegregation
chapters.summary: Burns describes her son's participation in the 1965 first-grade cohort, approximately sixteen children, that desegregated Tchula Consolidated School after a federal court ordered Holmes County to integrate. She recounts the school being burned in stages as white residents discovered Black children would attend, until the white population withdrew entirely to establish what became Cruger-Tchula Academy. Christopher Epps was among her son's classmates.
related_events: 1965 federal school desegregation order in Holmes County

chapters.title: Teaching Return and College Aspirations
chapters.summary: Burns returned to teaching in 1967 after a two-year absence and describes the gradual shift in students' college destinations from historically Black institutions like Mississippi Valley State and Jackson State toward the previously all-white Ole Miss. She notes Antoine Patrick of Tchula as one local resident who completed his degree there.
related_events: James Meredith's integration of Ole Miss (1962)

chapters.title: Holmes County School Board Service
chapters.summary: Burns ran for the Holmes County School Board around 1971 against incumbent Martin Smith, who died the night before the election. She served until approximately 1975 or 1976 with Dr. Arenia C. Mallory of Saints Industrial School as one of two Black members on a five-member board. She describes hiring younger white teachers as faculty integrated, and notes that William Dean became the county's first Black superintendent in the late 1980s.
related_events: Black electoral politics in Mississippi post-Voting Rights Act

chapters.title: Holmes County Health Research Program
chapters.summary: Burns served as secretary of the Mileston Cooperative, which funded the Holmes County Health Research Program led by University of Illinois professor Dr. Demitri Shimkin. The door-to-door health surveys documented unexpected hypertension rates, including in a six-year-old child, and the work informed the Robert F. Kennedy Economic Development initiative and the school breakfast and lunch program. The research also documented Black migration from plantations into apartment housing in Lexington, Pickens, Goodman, and Durant, and northward to Chicago, Peoria, and Wisconsin.
related_events: University of Illinois community health research

chapters.title: French Teaching and Continuing Career
chapters.summary: After leaving the school board, Burns taught English and literature at Mississippi Valley State until 1976, then returned to the Holmes County public schools. When Mississippi mandated foreign-language coursework for state-college admission, she added French certification through summer study at Delta State and continued teaching French part-time after her 2006 retirement.

loc_video.caption: Julia Matilda Burns oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Tchula, Mississippi, 2013 March 13
